enrichment.short_description: Flux is a free, open-source, CNCF Graduated GitOps tool that syncs Kubernetes clusters with the state declared in Git.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Flux is a free, open-source CNCF Graduated GitOps tool for Kubernetes, syncing clusters with Git and supporting Helm, Kustomize, and Flagger.

features.title: GitOps synchronization
features.description: Declarative infrastructure and application state stored in Git is automatically and continuously applied to Kubernetes clusters.

features.title: Progressive delivery
features.description: Integrates with Flagger for canary releases, feature flags, and A/B testing on top of GitOps-managed deployments.

features.title: Multi-cluster management
features.description: Manages applications and configuration across multiple Kubernetes clusters from Git.

features.title: Automated image updates
features.description: Scans container registries and can patch image tags, pushing the change back to Git automatically.

features.title: Pull-based security model
features.description: Uses a pull-based architecture with RBAC and least-privilege principles rather than exposing cluster credentials to CI systems.

features.title: Broad ecosystem support
features.description: Works with Kustomize, Helm, GitHub, GitLab, Slack notifications, OPA, and Kyverno.

pros: Free and fully open source
CNCF Graduated project status signals maturity and broad governance
Pull-based architecture reduces the security exposure of cluster credentials
Integrates with Flagger for progressive delivery (canary, A/B testing)
Supports multiple Git providers and Kubernetes distributions
cons: Requires working knowledge of Kubernetes and GitOps concepts to configure
No official managed/hosted offering — you operate it yourself
Progressive delivery features require installing the separate Flagger project

faqs.question: Is Flux free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Flux is fully open source and free to use.

faqs.question: Is Flux a CNCF project?
faqs.answer: Yes, Flux is a CNCF Graduated project.

faqs.question: Who created Flux?
faqs.answer: Flux was originally created by Weaveworks and is now developed as a community-driven project under CNCF governance.

faqs.question: Does Flux support canary deployments?
faqs.answer: Yes, through integration with Flagger, a separate progressive delivery tool in the Flux ecosystem.

faqs.question: Which Git providers does Flux support?
faqs.answer: GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and S3-compatible buckets.

use_cases.title: GitOps continuous delivery
use_cases.description: Automatically sync Kubernetes cluster state with a Git repository as the single source of truth.

use_cases.title: Multi-cluster application rollout
use_cases.description: Manage configuration and deployments consistently across multiple Kubernetes clusters.
use_cases.audience: DevOps teams operating fleets of clusters

use_cases.title: Automated image patching
use_cases.description: Automatically detect new container image versions and update Git manifests without manual intervention.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Flux?
paragraphs: Flux is a set of open-source, CNCF Graduated continuous and progressive delivery tools for Kubernetes. It implements GitOps: the desired state of applications and infrastructure is declared in a Git repository, and Flux continuously reconciles the live cluster to match it.

Originally created by Weaveworks, Flux is now a community-driven project governed under the Cloud Native Computing Foundation, with support for Kustomize, Helm, multiple Git providers, and integration with Flagger for progressive delivery such as canary releases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Flux is for
paragraphs: Flux is built for platform and DevOps teams running Kubernetes who want to manage cluster state declaratively through Git rather than manual kubectl or CI-driven deployments, particularly teams operating multiple clusters or requiring auditable, pull-based deployment security.

enrichment.short_description: Rancher is SUSE's open-source platform for managing Kubernetes clusters across cloud, on-prem, and edge infrastructure.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Rancher is a free, open-source Kubernetes management platform from SUSE, with Rancher Prime offering paid enterprise support.

features.title: Multi-cluster Kubernetes management
features.description: Manage clusters across cloud, on-premises, and edge infrastructure from a unified platform.

features.title: Zero-trust security policies
features.description: Container lifecycle security and policy controls across managed clusters.

features.title: Certified Kubernetes distributions
features.description: Provides certified, secure Kubernetes distributions for sensitive and edge deployments.

features.title: Unified operations dashboard
features.description: Central view for managing OS, storage, VMs, and containers across the fleet.

features.title: Developer workflow tooling
features.description: Tools to streamline application deployment and improve developer reliability.

pros: Free, open-source core with an active GitHub community
Backed by SUSE, an established enterprise software vendor
Manages Kubernetes clusters across multiple clouds, on-prem, and edge from one control plane
Cited by SUSE as a Leader in the 2025 Gartner Magic Quadrant for Container Management
Used by 30,000+ teams according to the vendor
cons: Rancher Prime (enterprise) pricing is not publicly listed and requires contacting sales
Requires Kubernetes expertise to deploy and operate effectively
Full enterprise feature set is gated behind the paid Prime offering

faqs.question: Is Rancher free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the open-source version of Rancher is free. SUSE also offers Rancher Prime, a paid enterprise edition with support.

faqs.question: Who owns Rancher?
faqs.answer: Rancher is developed by SUSE.

faqs.question: How many teams use Rancher?
faqs.answer: SUSE states more than 30,000 teams use Rancher.

faqs.question: Does Rancher work across multiple clouds?
faqs.answer: Yes, it manages Kubernetes clusters across cloud, on-premises, and edge environments.

use_cases.title: Enterprise Kubernetes fleet management
use_cases.description: Centrally manage and secure Kubernetes clusters across multiple environments.

use_cases.title: Edge and OT Kubernetes deployment
use_cases.description: Run certified Kubernetes distributions in industrial and edge settings.

use_cases.title: Multi-cloud cluster governance
use_cases.description: Apply consistent security and access policy across clusters spanning multiple cloud providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Rancher?
paragraphs: Rancher is an open-source platform for managing Kubernetes clusters, developed by SUSE. It provides a unified dashboard for deploying, securing, and operating containerized workloads across cloud, on-premises, and edge infrastructure.
The open-source core is free to use, while SUSE also sells Rancher Prime, a paid enterprise edition that adds vendor support, certified distributions, and a managed offering on AWS.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Rancher is for
paragraphs: Rancher is aimed at platform and DevOps teams responsible for operating Kubernetes at scale across multiple clusters and environments, from cloud-native infrastructure teams to industrial and edge deployments that need centralized policy and security controls.

enrichment.short_description: Portainer manages Docker and Kubernetes environments, with a free Community Edition and paid Business Edition plans.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Portainer manages Docker and Kubernetes containers with a free Community Edition and Business Edition plans starting at $105/month.

features.title: Multi-cluster fleet management
features.description: Manage containers across Kubernetes distributions including EKS, AKS, GKE, bare metal, and on-premises.

features.title: GitOps deployment workflows
features.description: Centralized reconciliation and promotion gates for Git-driven deployments.

features.title: Self-service deployment templates
features.description: Pre-defined templates with resource quotas and role-based access control.

features.title: Enterprise identity integration
features.description: SSO, LDAP, and OIDC support for centralized access control.

features.title: Air-gapped and offline support
features.description: Deployment support for edge and remote environments without internet access.

features.title: OT/industrial platform support
features.description: Works with operational technology vendors such as Ignition and Critical Manufacturing.

pros: Business Edition is free forever for up to 3 nodes
Works across both Docker and multiple Kubernetes distributions (Rancher, K3s, OpenShift, Talos)
Used by organizations including NASA and the U.S. Navy, per the vendor
Annual billing saves 16% compared to monthly on IT plans
Dedicated low-cost Home & Student plan for non-commercial use
cons: Enterprise plan pricing is not public and requires contacting sales
Starter and Scale plans restrict eligibility by organization revenue (≤$50M / ≤$100M)
Only community support is included on the entry-level Starter plan

faqs.question: Is Portainer free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Portainer Business Edition is free forever for up to 3 nodes.

faqs.question: How much does Portainer cost beyond the free tier?
faqs.answer: Enterprise IT plans start from $105/month for the Starter plan (5-15 nodes).

faqs.question: Does Portainer support Kubernetes and Docker?
faqs.answer: Yes, Portainer manages both Docker and Kubernetes environments.

faqs.question: Is there a plan for personal or student use?
faqs.answer: Yes, a Home & Student plan is available for $155/year for non-commercial use on up to 15 nodes.

use_cases.title: Container fleet management
use_cases.description: Manage Docker and Kubernetes containers across multiple environments from one dashboard.

use_cases.title: Industrial/OT container deployment
use_cases.description: Deploy and manage containerized workloads in manufacturing, retail, and field operations.

use_cases.title: Personal or lab container management
use_cases.description: Manage small self-hosted container setups with the Home & Student plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Portainer?
paragraphs: Portainer is an operational control platform for managing Docker and Kubernetes container environments. It provides a dashboard for deploying, monitoring, and securing containerized workloads without requiring deep cloud-native expertise.

Portainer offers a free Community Edition and a Business Edition that is free forever for up to 3 nodes, plus paid Enterprise IT plans (Starter, Scale, Enterprise) and a discounted Home & Student plan for non-commercial use.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Portainer is for
paragraphs: Portainer suits teams operating Docker or Kubernetes who want a centralized management dashboard, ranging from small teams using the free tier to enterprises and industrial/OT organizations needing SSO, RBAC, and air-gapped deployment support.

enrichment.short_description: Coolify is a free, open-source, self-hostable alternative to Heroku and Vercel for deploying apps to your own servers.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Coolify is a free, open-source self-hosted PaaS alternative to Heroku and Vercel, with Coolify Cloud starting at $5/month.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Deploy to any server you own, including VPS, Raspberry Pi, or cloud providers, with no vendor lock-in.

features.title: 280+ one-click services
features.description: Deploy databases and popular services with one click, alongside apps in multiple programming languages.

features.title: Automatic backups and SSL
features.description: Automatic database backups and free SSL certificate management via Let's Encrypt.

features.title: Git integration
features.description: Connects with GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and Gitea for deployment workflows.

features.title: Browser-based terminal
features.description: Real-time terminal access to servers directly from the browser.

features.title: Team collaboration
features.description: Tools for teams to collaborate on deployments and infrastructure.

pros: Self-hosting is free forever and fully open source
No vendor lock-in — deploy to any server you own
Supports 280+ one-click services
Low-cost managed Coolify Cloud tier starting at $5/month
Supports multiple Git providers (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, Gitea)
cons: Self-hosted setup requires managing your own server infrastructure
Coolify Cloud still requires bringing your own servers to deploy to
Only community and limited email support on the base Cloud plan

faqs.question: Is Coolify free?
faqs.answer: Yes, self-hosting Coolify is free forever and open source.

faqs.question: What is Coolify Cloud?
faqs.answer: A managed control panel service starting at $5/month for 2 connected servers, with $3/month per additional server.

faqs.question: Do I need my own server to use Coolify?
faqs.answer: Yes, both the self-hosted and Cloud versions require you to bring your own servers, such as a VPS or cloud instance.

faqs.question: What can I deploy with Coolify?
faqs.answer: Over 280 one-click services plus custom applications in multiple programming languages.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted app deployment
use_cases.description: Deploy web apps and services to your own servers without PaaS vendor lock-in.

use_cases.title: Managed deployment via Coolify Cloud
use_cases.description: Use a hosted control panel to manage deployments to your own connected servers.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Coolify?
paragraphs: Coolify is an open-source, self-hostable alternative to Vercel, Heroku, Netlify, and Railway. It lets developers deploy applications and services to their own servers — VPS, Raspberry Pi, or cloud instances — while keeping full ownership of their infrastructure and data.

Self-hosting Coolify is free forever and open source. Coolify also offers Coolify Cloud, a managed control panel starting at $5/month for 2 connected servers, for teams who want less operational overhead while still deploying to their own infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Coolify is for
paragraphs: Coolify is built for developers and small teams who want the deployment experience of a PaaS like Heroku or Vercel without the vendor lock-in, whether they self-host the control panel themselves or use the low-cost Coolify Cloud offering.

enrichment.short_description: CapRover is a free, open-source, self-hosted PaaS for deploying Docker apps with one-click installs and auto HTTPS.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CapRover is a free, open-source self-hosted PaaS for Docker apps, with one-click installs, automatic HTTPS, and Swarm scaling.

features.title: One-click app deployment
features.description: Deploy popular services such as databases, WordPress, and monitoring tools in seconds.

features.title: Automatic HTTPS
features.description: Free TLS certificates via Let's Encrypt with automatic renewal.

features.title: Multi-language support
features.description: Deploy Node.js, Python, PHP, Java, Ruby, .NET, or any container-based app.

features.title: Flexible deployment methods
features.description: Deploy via the web dashboard, CLI, webhooks, or Git workflows.

features.title: Docker Swarm scaling
features.description: Add nodes to a Docker Swarm cluster for load balancing and horizontal scaling.

pros: Completely free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license
No vendor lock-in — self-hosted on your own infrastructure
Simple one-click app catalog for common services
Automatic TLS certificate issuance and renewal
Runs on any infrastructure that supports Docker
cons: No official enterprise support or paid tier
You are responsible for managing and securing the underlying server(s)
Community-maintained, funded via OpenCollective donations rather than a company

faqs.question: Is CapRover free?
faqs.answer: Yes, CapRover is completely free and open source, licensed under Apache 2.0.

faqs.question: What is CapRover built on?
faqs.answer: Docker, nginx, Let's Encrypt, and Docker Swarm.

faqs.question: Does CapRover support scaling?
faqs.answer: Yes, via Docker Swarm nodes for load balancing and clustering.

faqs.question: How do I deploy apps with CapRover?
faqs.answer: Via the web dashboard, CLI, webhooks, or Git push.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted PaaS for solo developers
use_cases.description: Deploy and manage personal projects on owned infrastructure without a hosting bill per app.

use_cases.title: Small team app hosting
use_cases.description: Run multiple containerized apps and services without vendor lock-in.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is CapRover?
paragraphs: CapRover is a free, open-source, self-hosted Platform-as-a-Service that simplifies deploying and managing Docker-based applications. It is built on Docker, nginx, Let's Encrypt, and Docker Swarm.

It offers one-click deployment of popular services, automatic HTTPS certificate management, and support for common languages and frameworks, all while keeping full ownership of the underlying infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who CapRover is for
paragraphs: CapRover is aimed at developers and small teams who want a Heroku-like deployment experience on their own servers, without paying for a managed PaaS or dealing with complex container orchestration setup.

enrichment.short_description: Dokku is a free, open-source, Docker-powered PaaS that deploys Heroku-compatible apps via Git push to your own server.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dokku is a free, open-source Docker PaaS for Git-push deployment, with an optional paid Dokku Pro dashboard add-on.

features.title: Git-based deployment
features.description: Push Heroku-compatible applications to your server via Git.

features.title: Docker-powered isolation
features.description: Runs applications in isolated containers using Heroku buildpacks.

features.title: Extensible plugin system
features.description: Write and install plugins in any language to customize functionality.

features.title: No vendor lock-in
features.description: Install on any hardware or inexpensive cloud provider you control.

features.title: Dokku Pro dashboard (optional)
features.description: A paid, self-hosted web dashboard adds a management UI, JSON API, log streaming, and scaling controls on top of core Dokku.

pros: Core Dokku is free and open source
Git-push deployment workflow familiar to Heroku users
Highly extensible via community plugins
Can be installed on any Docker-compatible infrastructure, including generic Linux, Azure, DigitalOcean, and DreamHost Cloud
Optional Dokku Pro adds a management UI without requiring a subscription (one-time purchase)
cons: Core Dokku has no built-in web dashboard unless Dokku Pro is purchased separately
Dokku Pro costs $849 as an early-bird one-time purchase, with prices set to increase later
Requires self-managing the underlying server

faqs.question: Is Dokku free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core open-source Dokku is free. An optional paid add-on called Dokku Pro provides a management dashboard.

faqs.question: How much does Dokku Pro cost?
faqs.answer: Dokku Pro is offered at an early-bird one-time price of $849, covering one production server plus two pre-production environments.

faqs.question: What does Dokku deploy?
faqs.answer: Heroku-compatible applications, deployed via Git push and run in Docker containers using buildpacks.

faqs.question: Can Dokku be extended?
faqs.answer: Yes, through a plugin system that supports plugins written in any language.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted Heroku alternative
use_cases.description: Deploy apps via git push to a self-managed server instead of paying for managed PaaS hosting.

use_cases.title: Managed dashboard for Dokku fleets
use_cases.description: Use Dokku Pro's web UI, API, and log streaming to operate Dokku across production and pre-production environments.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Dokku?
paragraphs: Dokku is a free, open-source, Docker-powered Platform-as-a-Service, often described as the smallest PaaS implementation available. It lets developers push Heroku-compatible applications to their own server via Git, where they run in isolated containers built from Heroku buildpacks.

The core project is free and extensible through a plugin system in any language. A separate paid product, Dokku Pro, adds a self-hosted web dashboard, JSON API, log streaming, and scaling controls for teams that want a management UI on top of core Dokku.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Dokku is for
paragraphs: Dokku suits developers and small teams who want a lightweight, self-hosted alternative to Heroku, deploying via familiar Git-push workflows on their own infrastructure without recurring PaaS hosting fees.

name: Flynn
skip_reason: Project is discontinued/abandoned: flynn.io is unreachable (DNS resolution fails), and the official GitHub repo (github.com/flynn/flynn) is archived and explicitly labeled '[UNMAINTAINED]' since Sep 2021, with maintainers advising migration to other platforms.

enrichment.short_description: Tsuru is a free, open-source Platform as a Service built on Kubernetes for deploying apps in any language.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tsuru is a free, open-source PaaS built on Kubernetes, letting teams deploy apps in any language without managing infrastructure.

features.title: Language-agnostic deployment
features.description: Supports applications written in any programming language or framework.

features.title: Single-command deploys
features.description: Deploy applications using a simplified, single-command workflow.

features.title: Dynamic resource allocation
features.description: Scales and allocates compute resources dynamically to running applications.

features.title: Built on Kubernetes
features.description: Runs on Cloud Native Computing Foundation technologies, using Kubernetes as its underlying infrastructure.

features.title: Multi-cluster, multi-region management
features.description: Manages applications across multiple Kubernetes clusters and regions from a centralized control point.

pros: Free and open source
Built on standard Kubernetes/CNCF technologies rather than a proprietary runtime
Used in production by organizations including Globo.com, Jumia, and Stone, per the Tsuru website
Supports multi-cluster and multi-region deployments from one control point
Can be tried locally via Minikube
cons: Requires Kubernetes infrastructure to operate
Documentation and adoption skew toward Globo.com's own production use case
Smaller community compared to larger PaaS/GitOps projects

faqs.question: Is Tsuru free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tsuru is open source and free to self-host.

faqs.question: What does Tsuru run on?
faqs.answer: Kubernetes clusters, built on Cloud Native Computing Foundation technologies.

faqs.question: Who uses Tsuru?
faqs.answer: Organizations including Globo.com, Jumia, Stone, and Hotel Urbano, according to the Tsuru website.

faqs.question: Can I try Tsuru locally?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports local deployment using Minikube.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted PaaS on Kubernetes
use_cases.description: Standardize application deployment across teams on top of existing Kubernetes infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Multi-region application deployment
use_cases.description: Deploy and manage applications across distributed Kubernetes clusters and regions.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Tsuru?
paragraphs: Tsuru is an open-source Platform as a Service focused on developer productivity. It lets teams deploy applications in any programming language or framework across Kubernetes clusters without directly managing infrastructure.

Built on Cloud Native Computing Foundation technologies, Tsuru supports dynamic resource allocation, single-command deployments, and multi-cluster, multi-region management from a centralized control point. It is associated with Globo.com and used by organizations such as Jumia and Stone, per the Tsuru website.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Tsuru is for
paragraphs: Tsuru is suited to platform engineering teams that already run Kubernetes and want to give developers a simplified, language-agnostic deployment workflow across multiple clusters and regions.

enrichment.short_description: Cloudron is a self-hosting platform for web apps with automatic updates and backups, offering free and paid plans.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cloudron is a self-hosting app platform with automatic updates, backups, and email, offering a free plan and paid Pro/Max tiers from €15/month.

features.title: App Store deployment
features.description: Deploy production-ready apps from the Cloudron App Store.

features.title: Fast security patching
features.description: Security patches are released within 24 hours for apps and the server, per the vendor.

features.title: Centralized user management
features.description: Single dashboard for access control across multiple users and apps.

features.title: Built-in email server
features.description: Mail server with calendar, contacts, and spam filtering included.

features.title: Encrypted backups
features.description: Encrypted backups to cloud storage providers including S3, Google Cloud, and DigitalOcean.

features.title: App portability
features.description: Apps can be migrated across servers without complicated manual steps.

pros: Free plan available for running up to two apps
Fast security patch turnaround (within 24 hours, per the vendor)
Bundles email, backups, firewall, and SSO across all paid plans
Encrypted backups to multiple cloud storage providers
Apps can be migrated between servers
cons: Free plan is limited to two apps
Unlimited apps require a paid Pro or Max subscription
Advanced features like user groups/roles and a directory server require the top Max plan

faqs.question: Is Cloudron free?
faqs.answer: Cloudron offers a free plan limited to two apps; unlimited apps require a paid Pro or Max plan.

faqs.question: What does Cloudron's Pro plan cost?
faqs.answer: The Pro plan is €15/month on monthly billing, with unlimited apps.

faqs.question: What does the Max plan add?
faqs.answer: The Max plan (€25/month) adds user groups and roles, a directory server, and multiple backup sites.

faqs.question: Does Cloudron include email hosting?
faqs.answer: Yes, a built-in mail server with calendar, contacts, and spam filtering is included on all plans.

use_cases.title: Self-hosting a small app suite
use_cases.description: Run a limited set of self-hosted apps for free before upgrading.

use_cases.title: Full self-hosted office/collaboration stack
use_cases.description: Run unlimited apps, email, and backups on a personal or organizational server.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Cloudron?
paragraphs: Cloudron is a self-hosting platform that simplifies running web applications on your own server. It handles system administration tasks such as updates, backups, and security patching so users can focus on using apps rather than maintaining infrastructure.

Cloudron offers a free plan for up to two apps, and paid Pro and Max subscription plans for unlimited apps, with the Max plan adding user groups/roles, a directory server, and multiple backup destinations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Cloudron is for
paragraphs: Cloudron is aimed at individuals, small businesses, and privacy-conscious organizations who want to self-host a suite of web apps and email without manually managing server updates, backups, and security patches.

enrichment.short_description: YunoHost is a free, open-source, self-hosted server system for installing and managing apps with minimal expertise.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: YunoHost is a free, open-source self-hosting platform that simplifies installing and managing apps like email, cloud storage, and websites.

features.title: App catalog
features.description: Dedicated catalog of pre-configured apps installable through the web interface.

features.title: Email hosting
features.description: Self-hosted email with contact and calendar synchronization.

features.title: Cloud storage and file sharing
features.description: Host your own file storage and sharing services.

features.title: Web hosting
features.description: Host websites directly from a YunoHost server.

features.title: Admin web panel
features.description: Guided setup, app installation, and server maintenance through a web-based admin panel.

features.title: Broad hardware support
features.description: Installs on ARM boards, Raspberry Pi devices, repurposed computers, and VPS.

pros: Fully free and open source
Non-profit project maintained by volunteers, not a commercial vendor
Designed to work with minimal technical expertise
Runs on inexpensive hardware including Raspberry Pi and repurposed computers
Supported by sponsors including NLnet, NGI, and Code Lutin
cons: Community/volunteer-maintained rather than commercially supported
No official managed hosting offering — you must run your own hardware or VPS
App catalog quality and update cadence depend on individual app packagers

faqs.question: Is YunoHost free?
faqs.answer: Yes, YunoHost is entirely free and open source.

faqs.question: What hardware can run YunoHost?
faqs.answer: ARM boards, Raspberry Pi devices, repurposed computers, and virtual private servers.

faqs.question: Who maintains YunoHost?
faqs.answer: It is developed and maintained by volunteers as a non-profit project, supported by sponsors including NLnet, NGI, and Code Lutin.

faqs.question: Do I need technical expertise to use YunoHost?
faqs.answer: No, it is designed to let users install and maintain digital services with very little technical knowledge.

use_cases.title: Personal self-hosted server
use_cases.description: Run your own email, file storage, and website hosting on owned hardware.

use_cases.title: Low-cost self-hosting on small hardware
use_cases.description: Deploy a self-hosted app suite on inexpensive hardware like a Raspberry Pi.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is YunoHost?
paragraphs: YunoHost is a free, open-source system that lets users install and maintain digital services — apps they fully control — with very little technical knowledge. It is a non-profit, volunteer-maintained project.

Through a guided admin web panel and an app catalog, YunoHost supports self-hosted email, cloud storage, collaborative document editing, website hosting, and team communication tools, installable on ARM boards, Raspberry Pi devices, repurposed computers, or a VPS.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who YunoHost is for
paragraphs: YunoHost is aimed at individuals and small groups who want to self-host their own digital services on low-cost hardware without needing deep sysadmin skills, valuing data ownership and control over convenience of a commercial cloud provider.
